"The blossoming of the flower is a manifestation of universal law flowing through the plant; it is the will of the Infinite made manifest in the finite.  Illumination is the blossoming of the soul in man; it is just as natural, simple, and inevitable as the flowering of the rose. That's from Manly P. Hall's Self-Unfoldment by Disciplines of Realization, arguably Hall's best book and one of the classics of the Golden Age of American occultism.    

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. comments.content:
"Synchronicity" was not really part of my vocabulary before your post on the topic, so I'm very ignorant about it. Meanwhile I've seen a lot of discussion here of Neopagans and others who think that they're serving gods but, people here believe, may actually be serving something else.

Can a synchronicity that seems to be leading someone to aid and/or to a relationship with a god, actually be leading them into the control of malign entities? (Like Beckett's claim of a relationship with the Morrigan which a commenter here mocked?)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What determines the grade of entity you contact is your own state of consciousness.  If your mind's in a grubby place, you're not going to contact anything less grubby, while if you raise your state of consciousness through spiritual practice, you end up keeping a better grade of company. That's what leads people to think they're serving gods when they may be hanging out with something considerably less positive.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks, I'm taking that as a yes.

Follow-up:

Last week someone said something like, Beckett can't really be serving the Morrigan because the Morrigan is always bad. Or, I've seen someone say that the god they serve had agreed not to call on them in one specific area because they had past trauma in that area; someone else might say, "That's not really that god because that god doesn't make that type of agreement."

Is this type of reasoning ever accurate? The people making these arguments don't know the state of consciousness of the people they're seeing claim to have contacted a god. They seem to be reasoning backward from traits of the entity the person is contacting: "That god doesn't act that way, so that entity's not a god, so (I guess) that person's state of consciousness must not be right." Are they right? If someone hears themselves being discussed in this way, would it be a good idea to rethink their state of consciousness?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's difficult to judge another person's state of consciousness, and when (say) political disagreements are also involved, that makes things considerably harder! The things to look out for, when you're trying to assess whether a person might be in contact with a malign entity (or simply a low-grade spook pretending to be something much more important) are these. Flattery is the first; false predictions are the second; and the third is that the entity parrots the person's political or social agenda. Any one of those signs is reason to back away fast.

Hi Rusty,

For what it's worth, I've read "Appleseed" aloud almost daily for several weeks now. At first I had some of the emotional patterns others expressed here-- a bit of healthy weeping, etc-- but that went away after a short time, and within a few days was replaced by a giddy joy. I came to realize that the text is intrinsically playful, whimsical, and good humored, through and through, and I actually find the death scene the complete opposite of sad each time I read it-- rather uplifting actually, filled with a sense of deep satisfaction. Fits very well with JMG's notion of ascending into Gwynfydd. It's now at the point that reading the poem is mainly just a pleasant experience each day; I am still delighted to occasionally discover new facets to the imagery as I read.

By the way, the early version of the poem that got posted here last week is substantially different from the version that JMG used for the working. It goes full on "The Congo" (and then some) with its portrayal of the red savages bowing down before Johnny Appleseed as a god walking among them. (Honestly made me a little uncomfortable, but I get it, different times and all that.) Anyway, I found the revisions which became JMG's version-- including incorporating the rhyming "reading notes" into the actual body of the poem-- welcome changes. The one thing I did like better about the first version, though, was the way that the reading notes really turn the action of reading the poem into a sensorial imaginal act that engages not only inner sight, sound, and feeling, but also emotion.

P.S. Minor synchronicity alert-- my grandparents lived in the same neighborhood as Philip José Farmer in Peoria, Illinois, and used to play cards with him and his wife occasionally.

comments.title: Books on Greek Deities...
comments.content:
Last Monday, Kimberly Steele had asked about book titles on the Greek gods. I missed the window to reply then, and I hope Kimberly and JMG find this late reply helpful.

In terms of classical sources, Ovid’s 'Metamorphoses' is a primary source from which many contemporary retellings derive. It’s a good read in its own right, and I think it has more feeling than most modern retellings; Ovid respected the belief people had in these myths. Another source which is relatively comprehensive, though rather dry and terse, is Apollodorus’s 'Library of Greek Mythology' (it’s a book). There is a classical Roman discussion of the gods by Cicero, 'The Nature of the Gods' (and is bound in the same volume with 'On Divination').

A excellent contemporary retelling is 'The Wisdom of the Myths, How Greek Myths can Change Your Life', by Luc Ferry. He retells a large swath of the myths, helpfully organized into linked themes. He gets into some helpful exegesis. As good as the book is, there are problems: Luc Ferry is an atheist, and argues that the ancient Greeks were, to all intents and purposes, atheist, as the myths really don’t get into the afterlife. But he neglects the explicit devotional stances of Socrates/Plato, and ignores the development of Hellenistic religious ideas as expressed in Neoplatonism. (Digression alert: Luc Ferry is real privileged schmuck. He is a former French Minister of Education, who last year opined that the police should gun down Yellow Vest protestors in the street. Here is a source on that:

https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-6570491/Police-use-shoot-kill-tactics-Yellow-Vest-protesters-former-education-minister-says.html )

I recently discovered a modern scholar and apparent devotee of the Greek gods, Karl Kerenyi, a Swiss of Hungarian origin (died 1973), who was prolific in his writings on the gods and goddesses. I understand he was a friend of, and collaborator with, Jung. Karenyi has at least 15 titles which include:

The Greek Gods
The Religion of the Greeks and Romans
Zeus and Hera, Archetypal Images of Father, Husband, and Wife
Hermes, Guide of Souls [applies to incarnated souls I understand]
Goddesses of Sun and Moon
Athene, Virgin and Mother in Greek Religion
Dionysus, Archetypal Image of indestructible Life

That’s my belated 2 cents.

BTW JMG, what’s your opinion of Kerenyi?

1) A paper was published this month about the source for most meridians in traditional Chinese medicine being structures in the physical body. Hiding in Plain Sight‐ancient Chinese anatomy (PDF available for download):
https://anatomypubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/ar.24503

2) The comments some time ago, from a reader, on diabetes having an etheric base made me think of the ghosts without legs. This mirrors the process of gangrene in diabetes, with the toes dying first, and then the gangrene going "up". And if this is true, then exercises to strengthen the etheric body could... be helpful in some situations, let's live this at that.

Thanks for the Magic Mondays!

replies.title: Re: On Meridians and Ghosts
replies.content:

comments.title: Locating a missing person with Geomancy
comments.content:
Hi JMG and Commentariat,

Here in Canada, we dispense Methadone to maintain folks with opioid addictions so they don't need to buy street drugs. These people come to see me regularly, sometimes once a day, and I get to know them. Some of them make money thru prostitution.
One such regular customer, a young lady, disappeared suddenly a couple of months ago. replies.title: Re: Questions
replies.content:
1) It's not a matter of imagining the light coming from inside oneself, but of being surrounded and filled with infinite light. That's why we work with specific, defined sources of light in the magical work.

2) I live in a second floor apartment and the building has a basement. I have no trouble working with the telluric current, and neither should you.

3) Many people who talk about the Age of Aquarius know nothing about the astrological symbolism. All that stuff about increased connectedness and communal attitudes -- those are Piscean traits, not Aquarian ones. The Aquarian influence is eccentric, individualistic, divergent, idealistic, intellectual; its planetary ruler is Uranus, the planet of individual uniqueness, of events that will never be repeated and ideas that no one else understands. In the age of Aquarius we can expect 2160 years of oddballs, weirdos, strange little groups off on their own tangents, and bizarre events that make little sense to anyone before or after; the great leaders of the age will be colorful individuals who give other people plenty of room to do things their own way.

4) Concentrating on negative outcomes, brooding about them over and over again, and coming up with ornate plans for dealing with them, is a good way to make them happen. By all means come up with a plan B if some project fails, and then file it away and spend your time imagining what you'll do if it succeeds and how you'll make the best of the results of victory.

Hello JMG,

I suspect that Arthurian legend first emerged as a continuation of Mithraism. Here is my scenario about how it may have happened: When the ruling classes of Roman Empire began to adopt Christianity as their official religion, some Roman military officials who were loyal to Mithraism decided to move their cult center to Britain which was distant enough from the administrative centers of Roman Empire. There, they also initiated some native Celtic tribal chiefs who were recruited to Roman army. When the Romans pulled their main forces back from Britain in the 5th century, the remaining initiates (Romano-Celtic chiefs) began to amalgamate Mithraism with native Celtic myths and legends. Finally, during or right after the Saxon invasion, these local initiates substituted the semi-mythical Arthur figure for Mithra figure.

Do you think this is a plausible scenario? Do you know of any resource that investigates this hypothesis or a similar one?

Regards,
Minervaphilos

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I may, I just now happen to be reading intently on the subject of Arthurian legend.

Lewis Spence's The Magic Arts in Celtic Britain discusses the Mithraic connection in Chapter XII, "The Cultus of Arthur." He concludes,

"That Mithraism powerfully influenced the Neo-Druidic or Arthurian cult there is considerable proof. That is was among those influences which in Britain made for the later complete acceptance of the Christian faith is certain." p. 157, Barnes & Noble reprint edition, 1993.

You might also check out Joseph Campbell's Masks of God: Vol IV Creative Mythology, which explores connections between Grail symbolism and Orphic, Mithraic, Gnostic, Celtic and Druidic traditions.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you for the resources. The similarities between these two traditions are striking, but I wonder if there is a direct, traceable line of transmission from Mithraism to Arthurian cult. That's the mystery that has been puzzling me recently.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You might find Jessie Weston's book From Ritual to Romance worth reading. She argues that the Grail romances are a garbled record of a Mystery initiation that had been preserved in northern Britain into the Middle Ages, so it's relevant to your thesis.

The thing I'd point out is that Roman Britain didn't stay culturally Celtic in any meaningful sense. It was fully Romanized, and Mithraism didn't have to be imported late on; there were already Mithraei (underground temples of Mithras) in various parts of Roman Britain, including in such outlying military posts as Segontium (now Caernarfon, Wales). Here's a good introductory article on British Mithraei, and here's one on the one in London, with pictures. So Mithraism was definitely there, and it's quite possible that it could have been preserved by Roman-British initiates in the areas that remained free of Saxon invaders.

This act alone has the most powerful action and some say is the only real action we can perform.
When allowed to flow it manifests as it should and likewise when stifled it manifests as it should not. We have a reciprocal relation 'ship' with it as we sail along its waters and as it fills our sails. Much of what is going on these days seems to be 'ships' forcing themselves against the current and attempting to disrupt humanities proper connection with it, thereby getting us off course.
This brings me to a question and observation that I have consistently pondered. It seems that we all have an aspect of charlatan, and some of the greatest occult figures certainly seem to reflect this. It is easy to see in many of the most notorious ones of late(Blavatsky, Crowley, Gurdjieff, Evola, Pike, Casteneda). I also see it in Plato, Iamblichus, Bruno, and Dee. I see it in myself a and I relate it to observing the world through ego rather than higher faculties. I find that I can glean tons of useful insight from charlatans without getting sucked into their nonsense. It is something I wonder about, yet people that I talk to seem to avoid anything and all if they consider someone to be a charlatan. They also tend to buy into the most charlatan filled figures of contemporary culture. I see this in our modern politics and social movements. Just wondering how JMG and others view charlatans.

Learning to deal with them constructively is part of learning how to deal with our own egos constructively.

replies.title: A Useful Practice
replies.content:
I am studying Buddhism. Here is one prayer that might be useful to you.

May all beings have happiness and the causes of happiness.
May all be free from sorrow and the causes of sorrow.
May all never be separated from the sacred happiness which is sorrowless.
May all live in equanimity, without too much attachment and too much aversion.

Here is one way you might practice this prayer:

Set aside some time.
Sit with your spine straight, and settle.
Say: this practice I’m about to do, may it benefit all sentient beings.
Take a couple of minutes to just breathe.

Consider that above your head there’s a cloud of white light embodying love, compassion, and wisdom.
Rays of light fill your body.

Consider yourself and all the others in similar situations: all those who are hoping to become pregnant, or dealing with a challenging pregnancy.

Say the words of the prayer aloud.
Consider that rays of light bless all those coping with these circumstances.

Sit again for a couple of minutes.

Before you stand up say:
Whatever merit there may be in this practice, I dedicate it for all sentient beings.

--------- -----------

The words of the prayer are used in many traditions of Buddhism. However, the practice is something I just wrote down in the past 15 minutes, thinking of you and your circumstances, and hoping this might be useful. comments.title: Affirmations
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A couple questions in regards to affirmations.

(1) You've said that you should do an affirmation for at least a year and a day to determine its success. I have been doing two affirmations for about ten months now in regards to my writing. I feel as though both have been successful. I also have been feeling of late that it is time for new affirmations. I don't need another two months to determine if the affirmations I've been doing have been a success, which often seemed the point of your year and a day rule. If I feel they have been successful and am feeling it's time for new ones, should I go for it even though it's only been ten months, or would you recommend continuing with the current ones until a full year has passed?

(2) I am having a bit of trouble figuring out my next affirmations. I am pretty confident I have one figured out. But I'm struggling with how to phrase a second one. Basically, what I want to deal with is the fact that I feel like I allow fear to be too big a guiding principle in many of my life decisions, major and minor, and I also often have trouble making those decisions. I am good at imagining the way things might go wrong or restrict me in some way. I often struggle with this in regards to my career (I have often wanted to change from my current job, but then I will get cold feet when an opportunity comes along; I also tend to place restrictions on opportunities, like wanting something not full time so I can continue to have additional time for my writing, other personal projects, and my occult practices, and otherwise feeling very picky as to whether something fits). I also have come toward the idea of having children from the perspective of thinking of all the ways it would limit my life and potentially negatively impact my marriage. These fears and uncertainties also arise in other, more minor decisions, and I am often not very decisive in general anyway.

There's a knot of personal and emotional issues that drives this. I have always wanted to please others, so I am often at pains not to choose wrong when others are involved. I have had confidence issues and so am worried about . . . not always failing, exactly, but oftentimes just appearing to be incompetent, or not knowing what I'm doing. For instance, I can get pretty uncomfortable with unfamiliar situations and with being required to do things that I am unfamiliar with or fear I lack competency in, rather than just being willing to dive in and learn, damn the consequences. So I am very good at not making decisions based on what I actually want, but more on what I don't want to happen to me--or in an effort to please someone else, regardless of how I actually feel.

I don't want to lose all of that; I think there is benefit in being cautious up to a point, and I like thinking through the way things might unfold in advance, pro and con. I want to make informed decisions, I don't want to be rash, and I like being thoughtful up to a point. But nor do I want to be paralyzed by indecision or to base my choices on fear rather than on potential positives.

I realize all that is a lot and probably I need to continue with more reflection and journaling, but I'm struggling to come up with an affirmation that makes some sense of all this and cuts to the heart of the matter. I have thought of something very simple like "I am powerful," but not sure that's quite right. If I wanted to do something specifically about how I make decisions, I'm struggling to put it in a positive frame (I keep coming back to "My decisions are not based on fear" or something like that, but that's a negative framing, so not kosher--but I'm having trouble flipping it to a positive framing!) And I also am wondering if I have not yet got to the core of the matter. It may need to be that I should focus more on confidence, my desire to please others, etc.

Any suggestions? I don't expect you to come up with the affirmation for me, but curious if you have any guidance on this.

replies.title: Re: Affirmations
replies.content:
1) Finish the year and the day. That's excellent training for the will, and it will also see to it that the effects of your current affirmations become stable, and won't unravel when you stop working with them.

2) Good! You're wrestling with the complexities of deciding what to affirm, and that shows that you're paying attention. Affirmations that begin "I am..." are very powerful, and if you can find a word to go with that, you're set. You also want active voice, not passive voice -- "my decisions are not based on fear" is passive as well as negative. You want to affirm yourself as doing something or being something. "I make wise, firm, and thoughtful decisions"
might be an example.
